diagrams.title: Contiguous Memory Allocation Overview
diagrams.description: A block diagram showing memory divided into two main sections: System Area (OS kernel) and User Area (user processes). The user area is shown as a single contiguous block allocated to processes.
elements: Horizontal line dividing OS and User memory
User section labeled 'User Memory - Contiguous Allocation'
Process blocks within user memory (Process A, Process B, Process C)
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the fundamental concept of contiguous memory allocation where each process gets a single continuous block of memory, distinct from non-contiguous schemes like paging.

diagrams.title: Fixed Partitioning
diagrams.description: Memory divided into fixed-size partitions of unequal sizes. Shows how processes are placed into available partitions.
elements: Memory block divided into 4 fixed partitions (Partition 1: 40MB, Partition 2: 80MB, Partition 3: 120MB, Partition 4: 60MB)
Process blocks of various sizes placed in partitions
Internal fragmentation shown as shaded unused areas within partitions
Free partitions shown as empty blocks
diagrams.purpose: Students learn about fixed (static) partitioning, understanding how memory is pre-divided and how internal fragmentation occurs when process size is less than partition size.

diagrams.title: Variable Partitioning
diagrams.description: Memory showing dynamic allocation with variable-sized holes and processes using different allocation strategies.
elements: Memory timeline showing: Free space (80KB) → Process P1 (30KB) allocated → Free hole (50KB) → Process P2 (40KB) allocated → Free hole (10KB) remaining
Arrow annotations showing 'Best Fit', 'First Fit', 'Worst Fit' placement options
diagrams.purpose: Students visualize how variable partitioning works and understand the concept of external fragmentation where free memory exists in scattered holes.

diagrams.title: Allocation Strategies Comparison
diagrams.description: Four separate memory representations showing how the same set of processes would be placed using different allocation strategies.
elements: Memory bank 1: First Fit - first hole large enough found from beginning
Memory bank 2: Next Fit - search continues from last allocated position
Memory bank 3: Best Fit - smallest hole that is large enough
Memory bank 4: Worst Fit - largest available hole
Arrows showing search direction and allocation point for each strategy
diagrams.purpose: Students compare the four major allocation strategies, understanding their search patterns and trade-offs in terms of speed and memory utilization.

diagrams.title: Internal vs External Fragmentation
diagrams.description: Side-by-side comparison showing the two types of memory fragmentation in contiguous allocation schemes.
elements: Left side: Fixed partition with internal fragmentation - shaded 'unused' area within a partition boundary, label 'Internal Fragmentation = Partition Size - Process Size'
Right side: Variable partitioning with external fragmentation - scattered small holes between allocated blocks, label 'External Fragmentation = Total Free Memory but not contiguous'
diagrams.purpose: Students clearly differentiate between internal fragmentation (wasted memory within allocated block) and external fragmentation (wasted free memory scattered throughout) and understand their implications.

charts.title: Fixed vs Variable Partitioning
charts.content: Comparison table with columns: Aspect | Fixed Partitioning | Variable Partitioning; Rows: Memory Division | Pre-divided at system boot | Dynamic as processes arrive; Partition Size | Fixed (may cause internal fragmentation) | Variable (matches process size); Number of Partitions | Limited by hardware (max N processes) | Limited only by total memory; External Fragmentation | No | Yes (compaction may be needed); Memory Utilization | Lower (internal fragmentation) | Higher; Complexity | Simple | Moderate; Process Loading | All processes must fit in partition | Any process up to max memory

charts.title: Allocation Strategies Comparison
charts.content: Comparison table: Strategy | Search Method | Advantages | Disadvantages; First Fit | Search from beginning, use first hole >= process | Fastest, low overhead | Creates many small holes; Next Fit | Search from last allocation point | Similar to First Fit, better distribution | May miss large holes at beginning; Best Fit | Search entire memory, use smallest fit | Minimizes wasted space | Slower (searches all), creates small holes; Worst Fit | Use largest hole available | Large leftover hole usable | Slower, may fragment large holes; Quick Fit | Maintain separate lists by size | Very fast allocation | Complex memory management

charts.title: Memory Allocation Decision Process
charts.content: Flowchart: Start → Is there any free hole? → No: Process must wait/termination → Yes: Is hole >= process size? → No: Search next hole → Yes: Allocate memory to process → Calculate fragmentation (internal/external) → Update memory list → End. Also shows decision points for selecting allocation strategy (First/Best/Worst/Next Fit).

charts.title: Fragmentation Types in Contiguous Allocation
charts.content: Classification chart: Fragmentation → Internal Fragmentation (caused by fixed partitions, defined as partition size - actual process size, cannot be reused) | External Fragmentation (caused by variable partitioning, total free memory > largest process but not contiguous, can be solved by compaction). Related: Compaction - moving all allocated processes to gather free memory into one block.

branches: Partitioning Methods (Fixed Partitioning - equal/unequal sizes, Variable Partitioning - dynamic holes)
Allocation Strategies (First Fit - first available, Next Fit - circular search, Best Fit - minimum fit, Worst Fit - maximum fit)
Fragmentation Types (Internal - wasted space within partition, External - scattered free holes)
Memory Management (Compaction, Memory Tables - bitmap, linked list)
Advantages (Simple, Fast access - no translation needed)
Disadvantages (Fragmentation, Limited flexibility, Memory must be contiguous)
mindMaps.purpose: Provides comprehensive overview of contiguous memory allocation covering partitioning methods, allocation strategies, fragmentation issues, and trade-offs. Helps students connect all concepts and understand why modern OS use paging/segmentation instead.
